notes:
README LEADS WITH FIVE SCREENS 2026-07-21 (session 26-07-21-013, impl commit 63bbb7a). Docs + assets, no source change, 195 tests unchanged. THE PROBLEM: 402 lines of accurate content in a shape that only works on a reader already convinced -- ~1.1k installs/month against zero stars is not a product signal, it is a document that never shows the tool working before the reader has scrolled past a wall of prose. FIVE SCREENS now sit above everything, one problem sentence + one image each: the gate blocking a lying push; the queue handing over the next slice AND refusing one already shipped; the plan checked as an EXECUTABLE PLAN not a list; the one-screen status with the roadmap scoreboard; a declared number that stopped being verified. Nothing deleted -- the 402 lines sit below the point a reader wants them. The queue and the facts layer, the two halves nobody discovered, are now unmissable instead of buried at section 00 and in the command table. THE ASSETS: six shots, real output byte-for-byte, captured on the PUBLISHED 0.14.1 against a demo cockpit whose queue and history are genuinely chained. No mockups, no AI-generated terminals -- the guide is absolute and the product reason is stronger than the rule: a README whose screenshots are generated, for a tool whose whole claim is prove-it-against-reality, refutes itself the first time somebody runs the command and sees a different screen. Produced by docs/img/capture-readme-shots.sh (committed) which REFUSES TO RUN unless casp --version matches the release it documents -- a screenshot on an older binary is a stale claim inside the one document arguing stale claims are the problem, and the guard is what stops that depending on memory. Optimisation is IN the same script: 20.7 MB of retina PNGs -> 272 KB, 60x at 1600px/64 colours, visually identical since terminal output has almost no palette; in the script rather than in a habit for the same reason as the version guard -- a manual step is the one skipped on the release where it mattered. Images referenced by absolute raw.githubusercontent.com URL rather than hosted on the project site: GitHub proxies README images, npm does NOT, so site-hosted images would mean every npm page view hitting a host this project runs, which sits badly beside 'nothing leaves your machine'. The README states the capture version (0.14.1) rather than 'the current version' -- a checkable claim that can go stale beats a vague one that can never be wrong, GIVEN something exists to stop it going stale. TWO STANDING CANON VIOLATIONS FIXED, pre-existing: 'the one gap NOTHING ELSE covers' and '(this is the gap NOTHING ELSE fills)' are the only/first/best family the positioning canon rules out -- the wedge is stated as a fact (validation vs storage, deterministic vs probabilistic), never as a superiority claim. NOTE: 0.14.1's orphan-storm fix came out of this work -- the first gate screenshot returned three warnings correct about the walk and wrong about the repository. Capturing the product honestly is what found the defect, the same way 0.13.0's chain bug surfaced. STILL PARKED: next_phase/next_prompt null.

0.14.1 SHIPPED 2026-07-21 (session 26-07-21-012, impl commit 863baaa): a SHIPPED HEAD buried its own finding. Found by DOGFOODING, not by reading code -- while capturing the README's proof screenshots against the demo cockpit (five phases shipped, a three-slice queue chained with next_after), the flagship drift shot (next_prompt pointing at an already-shipped slice) came back 26 PASS / 3 WARN / 1 FAIL. The three warnings claimed each queued prompt was unreachable from the head. They were not wrong about the WALK; they were wrong about the REPOSITORY -- that queue is coherent, 15->16->17, every link resolving. What is wrong is the head, and CASP-PROMPT-003 already said so one line above. THE DEFECT: 0.13.0 shipped a guard for exactly this and its own comment states the intent in full -- 'when next_prompt is missing OR ALREADY SHIPPED, CASP-PROMPT-001/003 already FAIL, and burying that one actionable finding under an orphan warning per queued prompt would make the report worse.' The condition implemented HALF that sentence: hasHead tested byRel.has(headRel), and byRel is built from EVERY prompt (src/chain.ts:282) including shipped ones. A head that existed but had already shipped satisfied hasHead, the reachability walk ran from a node nothing chains forward from, and every declaring queued prompt came back unreachable. Worst possible blast radius: next_prompt pointing at an already-shipped slice is the most common drift there is and the specific bug this product was built to catch, so the report that matters most was the one being degraded -- and it degrades in PROPORTION TO QUEUE LENGTH, so a repo that plans further ahead gets more noise, which is exactly backwards. FIX: a usable head means a prompt that can still RUN -- present AND queued/in-progress, not merely a file that exists. analysis.order is only assigned when the walk ran and the chain is coherent, so a shipped head now also yields queue: null in status --json rather than a partial order a consumer cannot tell is partial (the principle 0.13.0 established post-review when a false queue shipped). Same drift on the demo cockpit now reports 26 PASS / 0 WARN / 1 FAIL. WHY THE TEST MISSED IT: there WAS a test -- 'no usable head -> no orphan storm on top of the real finding' -- pointing next_prompt at GONE.md, a head that does not EXIST. The other half of the documented sentence, a head that exists but already shipped, was never exercised. A test that pins half a claim is indistinguishable from outside from one that pins all of it -- same shape as 0.14.0's consent-gate test, which asserted nothing was WRITTEN while nothing asserted nothing was RUN. 193 -> 195 tests. ALSO: docs/img/capture-readme-shots.sh -- the README's six proof screenshots are regenerated from a real cockpit by a committed script, and it REFUSES TO RUN unless casp --version matches the release it documents, because a screenshot taken on an older binary is a stale claim inside the one document arguing stale claims are the problem. The gate shot uses check --quiet: the full report opens with ~26 PASS lines and a wall of green above the one red line is how a reader misses the point; the flag is real and documented, so the shot stays an honest output. NOT YET PUBLISHED TO NPM -- 0.14.0 is latest; publishing 0.14.1 is a separate CEO-gated act, and the README screenshots must be captured on the PUBLISHED binary, so the publish gates the README work. STILL PARKED: next_phase/next_prompt null.

0.14.0 PUBLISHED 2026-07-21 (session 26-07-21-011, impl commit 8a46589). @justethales/casp@0.14.0 is now 'latest' on npm -- the facts layer, compare-and-swap on state writes, the fact-verify consent gate and the hostile-filesystem hardening all ship in that one entry. Verified AFTER publish by installing the published tarball into an empty directory rather than trusting the local build: binary reports 0.14.0, casp rules resolves all eight new codes (CASP-FACT-001..006, CASP-IO-001/002). CORRECTION TO THE NOTES BELOW: every prior entry says '0.11.0 through 0.14.0 remain unpublished'. That was wrong at the time it was written -- 0.11.0, 0.12.0 and 0.12.1 were published on 2026-07-21 before those notes were saved. The accurate statement was that 0.13.0 and 0.14.0 were unpublished. 0.13.0 IS DELIBERATELY NEVER PUBLISHED and the hole in the version sequence is intentional: the code tagged 0.13.0 in the changelog contained the aliased-fork defect, where status --json emitted a `queue` asserting an order the frontmatter did not support -- a false machine-readable claim, the worst failure class this product has, since a consumer cannot tell it is wrong. The fix landed in 03a92b0 and is folded into the 0.14.0 changelog entry. Publishing a version known to emit a false claim for the sake of contiguous numbers is not a trade this project makes. TWO DOCUMENTATION DEFECTS FIXED IN THE STATE SURFACE, no source file touched, 193 tests unchanged. (1) Four shipped prompts (PHASE-04-CLOSE-LOOP, PHASE-CASP-HELP, PHASE-CONFIGURABLE-PATHS, PHASE-FACTS-LAYER) carried session_id: pending beside a session_log pointer that resolved. check passed and was CORRECT -- CASP-PROMPT-005 requires the session_log pointer, and no rule reads session_id on a prompt -- so this is a documentation defect, not drift. But it is a documentation defect in the state surface, the one place the protocol asks to be literally true, and a field reading 'pending' next to the field naming the log that closed it is a contradiction a reader resolves by guessing. Backfilled from each prompt's own session_log stem; nothing invented. NOT turned into a rule: session_id on a prompt is derivable from session_log, and a rule enforcing agreement between a field and its own derivation argues for deleting the field instead. Left as a possible simplification, not queued. (2) PHASE-FACTS-LAYER.md was written in FRENCH -- the single French document in a public repository and published package whose every other prompt, doc, README and changelog entry is English, and the design spec for the release that just shipped, so the likeliest file to be opened from the 0.14.0 changelog. Flagged as an open question at the close of 0.13.0 and never decided. Translated in full with structure preserved: same sections, tables and severities, code blocks byte-identical except two prose strings inside the JSON example. No claim softened, no scope changed, the historical framing intact (still a spec drafted against 0.10.0 and refreshed at the close of 26-07-21-007). One line added rather than translated: the status banner read QUEUED while the frontmatter read shipped -- it now names the release. Rest of the public tree scanned for the same residue (docs/, README.md, CHANGELOG.md, session-logs/, templates/, skills/): none. STILL PARKED: next_phase/next_prompt null; the only phases_queued entry is demand-gated-tail, a holding placeholder whose own prompt forbids executing it as a session -- each item inside needs its own prompt and its own explicit trigger. Nothing in this session changes that.

0.14.0 HARDENED 2026-07-21 (session 26-07-21-010, impl commit 4996b86): hostile filesystem -- a gate that crashes is not a verdict. existsSync answers 'is there a name here', never 'can this process open it', so a file that existed and could not be READ threw out of whichever verb touched it. Reproduced against the built binary with one chmod 000 prompt: check printed a raw EACCES stack trace; check --json and status --json printed NOTHING while exiting 1; human status printed 411 bytes then died mid-report. The status --json row is the serious one -- docs/status-json.md documents that verb as never gating (exit 0 for any valid cockpit, drift included), so a consumer got a non-zero exit and an empty stdout from a contract saying neither can happen. Same class as the 0.13.0 false queue: a machine contract that silently does not hold. FIX: one door in src/shared.ts (readTextFile / readBytes / readDirEntries / pathKind / readFrontmatter / readStateFile) returning a discriminated result carrying the path and the OS errno -- scattering try/catch at each call site would work exactly once. readFrontmatter's SIGNATURE changed rather than being wrapped: a null-returning wrapper would let all eight call sites keep conflating 'cannot open' with 'no frontmatter', which is the bug. NEW CASP-IO-001 (content the gate needed is readable) and CASP-IO-002 (the run completed -- a backstop; a finding there is a CASP bug). Both FAIL: the gate may say 'I could not check this', never 'clean'. checkOneSafe guarantees check --json emits a parseable v1 document; status --json exits 0 as documented; doctor still 0; cli.ts prints one line, never a trace, and fails closed. ONE DELIBERATE BEHAVIOUR CHANGE: a directory named *.md in sessions_dir/logs_dir was SILENTLY SKIPPED and is now a FAIL -- a document CASP enumerated and could not read is exactly how a broken state surface reads as clean, and in sessions_dir no downstream rule catches the consequence. One existing test updated. THE AUDIT CAUGHT A REGRESSION THIS FIX INTRODUCED, BEFORE PUSH -- and this time the review ran before the push, unlike 0.12.0 and 0.13.0. Removing the old isFile() filter handed non-regular files to readFileSync, and readFileSync on a FIFO with no writer does not fail, it BLOCKS in open(2) forever. No try/catch catches a hang, and a gate that never returns is strictly worse than the stack trace it replaced: no verdict AND no exit code, so a pre-push hook wedges the terminal and CI runs out to its own timeout. Reads now stat before they open. Three more from the same review: an unreadable next_prompt inside sessions_dir was reported TWICE under one id (a consumer keying by id drops one; drift count read 2 for one defect) -- now deduplicated by path; 'vanished' was exempted as a silent skip, which in the logs walk empties the declared-phase set, collapses the adoption window and silences the whole CASP-SESSION-003 category, converting a real FAIL into no output -- no longer exempt; and status --json's assembly outside checkOneSafe could still throw before its single write, reproducing the original defect one function over -- now always emits a valid v1 document with check.verdict null and exit 0. docs/check-json.md and docs/status-json.md updated where the change made a documented contract stale. 179 -> 193 tests, schema stays v1, and check on this repo is byte-identical to the pre-session binary (diffed against a worktree build of 91c822b, both formats). METHODOLOGY NOTE: the first reproduction pass was thrown away -- it ran node $CLI $cmd unquoted under zsh, which does not word-split, so 'check --json' arrived as one argument and hit the unknown-command path, faking the exact symptom the real bug produces. Re-measured with each argument passed separately. NOT PUBLISHED TO NPM -- 0.11.0 through 0.14.0 remain stacked; publishing stays a separate CEO-gated act. PARKED AFTER THIS SHIP: next_phase/next_prompt set to null. The only item left in phases_queued is demand-gated-tail, a holding placeholder whose own prompt says DO NOT EXECUTE AS A SESSION and which needs an explicit CEO-validated trigger per item. Do not synthesize a next_prompt against it; wait for a real signal.

0.14.0 SHIPPED 2026-07-21 (session 26-07-21-008, impl commit bb7f71b): the facts layer -- proves FRESHNESS, never truth. Everything check has ever verified is a claim inside state.json against git; a real incident showed the cost of that gap -- five false claims lived in the documents ORBITING the cockpit (a unit cost never recalculated after its source config changed, a database row count read off a PostgreSQL planner estimate ~40x off, a percentage reconciling with no source) and check stayed green throughout, correctly, because none was state drift. THE REVERSAL: CASP cannot prove a claim TRUE, only that it stopped being VERIFIED -- has the source changed (hash), has the verification aged out (TTL), was a method ever recorded (presence). Three comparisons, zero LLM, same shape as migrations_applied. NEW casp/facts.json, opt-in exactly like CASP-SESSION-003 and the prompt-chain rules -- zero CASP-FACT-* findings, not even a PASS, until a cockpit scaffolds it. CASP-FACT-001..006: source resolves (FAIL) / source_hash matches current content (FAIL -- the rule that catches the founding case) / TTL not exceeded (WARN, FAIL past double; every fact requires ttl_days, even a hashed repo source) / used_in carries a <!-- casp:fact id --> marker checked for PRESENCE only, never the value around it (WARN) / method recorded (WARN) / method matches no known trap (FAIL). NEW src/traps.ts: static registry (n_live_tup/n_dead_tup without count(, EXPLAIN without ANALYZE, reltuples, docker stats --no-stream), project-extensible via a plain substring array in facts.json -- never a regex, since facts.json is repository content. NEW casp fact list|check|verify|stale -- verify <id> is the ONE deliberate code-execution surface in the binary: replays the declared method, shows before/after, requires confirmation before writing. FIX -- compare-and-swap on every state.json write: 0.12.1's atomicity protects a partial write, not an overwritten one, and two agents raced the same cockpit in the founding incident with the second silently winning. loadStateWithHash + an optional expectedHash on saveState now refuse (StateConflictError, nothing written) if the file changed since it was read; wired through close, ship, audit bump, upgrade. docs/what-casp-proves.md gets the sharper limit in its own voice: an unchanged hash and an unexpired TTL can both be right about freshness and wrong about substance if the original verification was itself wrong, and the trap registry catches only the traps it knows. 151 -> 176 tests, check --json schema stays v1, no existing finding changes verdict, a cockpit with no facts.json is provably unaffected (pinned by test). PARKED AFTER THIS SHIP: next_phase/next_prompt set to null. facts-layer was the whole remaining VALIDATED queue (see casp-sh/CLAUDE.md) -- the only item left in phases_queued is demand-gated-tail, a holding placeholder whose own prompt says DO NOT EXECUTE AS A SESSION (each item needs its own prompt + explicit CEO-validated trigger: CI status-check installer, casp notify --webhook, casp rollback, native binaries, casp timeline/metrics, slash-command distribution). Do not synthesize a next_prompt against that placeholder; wait for a real signal. NOT PUBLISHED TO NPM -- 0.11.0 through 0.14.0 are all stacked; publishing stays a separate CEO-gated act.

0.13.0 SHIPPED 2026-07-21 (session 26-07-21-007, impl commit c39b836): prompt-chain integrity, CASP-PROMPT-007..010 -- the first rules to validate the QUEUE as an ordered plan rather than its head. Every prior PROMPT/SESSION rule covered one prompt (the head named by next_prompt) or the past (what shipped); the ordering of what had not run yet was a template convention with no rule behind it, so a queue could be silently unexecutable while check exited 0. THE SPLIT: dangling next_after (007) and cycle incl. self-reference (008) FAIL because they are claims that CANNOT be true -- the plan is unexecutable as written; fork (009) and unreachable-from-head (010) WARN because they are AMBIGUOUS rather than false, and a deliberate parking lot of queued-but-unchained prompts is a legitimate way to work. Carried over from the phase prompt's recommendation unchanged. OPT-IN, DERIVED FROM THE DATA, NO STATE KEY: the canonical template ships next_after as a literal placeholder, so a rule firing on it would have reddened every cockpit already on CASP. A placeholder, an empty string, null, and ANY non-string YAML value (number, float, bool, list, map, ~, whitespace) are not declarations and produce nothing; a repo where no queued prompt declares a real predecessor emits no finding at all, not even a PASS -- the CASP-SESSION-003 treatment, now the established shape for an opt-in rule. Skipped non-declaring prompts are counted in the PASS detail, never silent. RESOLUTION IS EXACT AFTER A DOCUMENTED NORMALIZATION: prompt stem / its lowercase form / its slug (scaffold PHASE- prefix removed), a session id via the CASP-SESSION-001 resolver, or a phase id from state. No edit distance anywhere -- CASP-SESSION-003 refused fuzzy filename matching and refusing it twice is the point of having a precedent. Identity collisions (A.md and PHASE-A.md both answer to 'a') resolve by explicit precedence over a path-sorted list, NOT by readdir order -- the first implementation let the filesystem decide, which would have let the same repo chain differently on two machines. Only queued prompts are subjects; a shipped prompt stays a valid TARGET but its own next_after is never re-litigated. No orphan storm: when there is no usable head, CASP-PROMPT-001/003 already FAIL and the reachability walk stays silent rather than burying the one actionable finding. status --json gains `queue` (resolved order, head first; null when unadopted or incoherent) -- additive, both schemas stay v1, status still exits 0 on a drifted chain. DOGFOODING FOUND THE FIRST BUG IN THIS REPO: on its first real fixture the rule reported two CASP-PROMPT-010 warnings and they were correct -- PHASE-FACTS-LAYER declared next_after: check-shipped-log (shipped as 0.11.0) and PHASE-DEMAND-GATED-TAIL declared PHASE-VERIFY-AND-STATE-DIFF (shipped long before). Neither was dangling; both resolved cleanly and simply encoded no order. The chain existed on paper. Both corrected; the queue now resolves prompt-chain-integrity -> facts-layer -> demand-gated-tail. OUT-OF-SCOPE FIX, TAKEN DELIBERATELY: the commissioned review of this slice never returned a report, so the three priority checks were re-run by hand by EXECUTION rather than reading, and found a pre-existing crash -- a non-string next_prompt (42, a list, an object) reached join() and threw ERR_INVALID_ARG_TYPE, killing the ENTIRE report with a raw stack trace before any other check ran. A gate that crashes is worse than one that reddens: CI reads it as infrastructure failure, not drift. Now a CASP-PROMPT-001 FAIL naming the type. Same class as the 0.12.1 scalar-state crash; next and status already coerced through String(), check was the one unguarded verb. Other adversarial probes passed: missing//file-shaped sessions dir, a directory named *.md among the prompts, missing session-logs/, phases_shipped holding non-strings, next_prompt escaping via ../../../etc/passwd, and three disjoint cycles each reported separately with the upstream node correctly excluded from the ring it feeds. 121 -> 146 tests. check --json schema stays 1, status --json schema stays 1, no existing finding changes verdict, a cockpit scaffolded before this release still PASSes (pinned by test). PHASE-FACTS-LAYER.md REFRESHED at close: it was drafted 2026-07-20 against 0.10.0 and four releases had shipped since. Its SHOULD asked a future session to make saveState atomic -- ALREADY DONE in 0.12.1 (temp + rename), so a fresh agent would have re-implemented shipped work; the section now says so explicitly and re-scopes the remaining half, which is the compare-and-swap the incident actually exhibited (atomicity protects a partial write, not an overwritten one). Stale line references (README.md:285 pointed at unrelated prose after two README rewrites) replaced with section names. Added a what-changed-since-drafting table: 0.11.0 and 0.13.0 are the two working precedents for derived adoption, and 0.12.0 is what UNBLOCKS the phase since casp/facts.json is a new cockpit-root file no existing repo could have received before upgrade existed. OPEN QUESTION FOR THE CEO: PHASE-FACTS-LAYER.md is written in French while casp-core is a public repo and a published npm package and every other prompt is English -- left as-is, not a drive-by language flip. NOT PUBLISHED TO NPM -- 0.11.0, 0.12.0 and now 0.13.0 are stacked; publishing stays a separate CEO-gated act. POST-PUSH REVIEW FIXES (commit 03a92b0, folded into 0.13.0 which is unpublished, not a 0.13.1): the commissioned adversarial review returned AFTER the first push with GO-WITH-FIXES and one real BLOCKER. Fork detection was keyed on the RAW next_after string -- the one site in chain.ts that never got the identity treatment -- so `next_after: PHASE-A` and `next_after: phase-a` looked like two predecessors instead of one slice. The CASP-PROMPT-009 WARN was missed, nothing else was wrong, so the chain was declared COHERENT and status --json's `queue` published a linear order asserting C runs after B when C declares it runs after A. A FALSE MACHINE-READABLE CLAIM is worse than a missed warning: a consumer of `queue` cannot tell it is wrong, and `queue` is the one field this release added for agents to consume. Fixed by keying forks on the RESOLVED target -- every identity of a slice shares one canonical key (path for a prompt, the string itself for a terminal); messages print each claimant's own spelling so the report never shows a canonical name the operator never typed. Three smaller fixes same review: a cycle also emitted an orphan WARN per ring member (one defect reported N times, contradicting the missing-head guard's own principle); the skipped count lived only in the PASS line so it vanished exactly when the report was non-trivial, against the spec's explicit 'never silent' (a scope line is now emitted either way); and a stale comment describing the pre-tiering resolver. 146 -> 151 tests. PROCESS NOTE, SAME MISTAKE AS 0.12.0: the push went out before the review returned. Green build, passing tests, semantic defect. Recoverable only because nothing was published -- had npm publish been bundled into this session a false `queue` would have shipped. Do not push a new rule category ahead of its commissioned review.
